title: Cayenne warming oil for joints
subtitle: A skin-warming herb topical oil for mild aching joints
excerpt: A simple infused oil of dried cayenne pepper in olive oil, used as an external skin-warming herb rub for mild arthritic aching, cold stiff joints, and muscular tension. Warm to the touch from the capsaicin.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve (the early 20th-century botanical writer), A Modern Herbal, cayenne entry: the skin-warming herb and external stimulant tradition. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898), Capsicum, the external use for joint and muscle ache.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps 6-12 months in a dark glass bottle, cool and dark.
recipe.temperatureNote: Hold at 55-65°C. Below 50°C the extraction is poor; above 70°C the capsaicinoids degrade.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb that causes warming and reddening of the skin when applied externally. The word comes from the Latin rubefacere (to make red). Rubefacients bring blood to the surface and are traditionally used for cold, stiff, aching joints and muscles.

The primary active compound in cayenne pepper, responsible for the heat sensation and the rubefacient action. It depletes substance P in nerve endings on repeated application, which is the basis of pharmaceutical capsaicin preparations for chronic pain.

content.text: : an oil that warms the skin and brings blood to the surface on application. The

from the dried cayenne pepper dissolves into the olive oil during the low-heat soaking. The finished oil has a pronounced warming sensation when applied to the skin. It is appropriate for cold, stiff joints in cold weather, mild arthritic aching, and muscular tension; conditions where warmth is the therapeutic aim. It is external use only, and never applied to broken skin, mucous membranes, or near the eyes.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Eye and mucous membrane contact. Capsaicin causes significant pain on contact with the eyes, mucous membranes, or genitals. Wash hands thoroughly with soap after each application before touching the face. Do not apply near the eyes, mouth, or any mucous membrane.

Skin reaction. Some readers experience intense burning on first application. Test on a small area of the inner arm first. If the burning is severe or lasts more than 30 minutes, wash off immediately with soap and water.

Anticoagulants. Cayenne has antiplatelet activity at high oral doses; external application at this preparation's dose is unlikely to have significant systemic effect, but readers on anticoagulants should check with their prescriber before regular use over large areas.

Joint pain that needs medical attention. Swollen, hot, red joints (suggesting inflammatory arthritis, gout, or infection), joint pain following injury, or joint pain with fever needs medical assessment.

items.prepNote: dried cayenne pepper or dried chilli flakes; cayenne (Capsicum annuum) at standard culinary cayenne heat level
olive oil or sunflower oil; olive oil has a more stable shelf life

content.text: Combine the dried cayenne and olive oil in the top of a double boiler. Clip the probe thermometer in place. Bring the oil to 55-65°C and hold for 2 hours. The oil will turn deep red-orange and smell pungently of cayenne. Stir once an hour. Strain through a fine-mesh sieve lined with muslin, pressing the spent cayenne. Bottle in a small dark glass dropper bottle using a funnel. The oil should be brick-red and smell intensely of cayenne pepper.

Wash hands before applying. Apply a small amount (3-5 drops) to the affected area and massage in gently, avoiding mucous membranes and eyes. Wash hands immediately and thoroughly with soap. Apply up to three times a day. Test on a small area first; the warming sensation should be noticeable but not unbearable. If the skin becomes significantly inflamed, blistered, or very sore, wash off immediately and discontinue use.
Small dark glass dropper bottles, sealed, cool and dark. Keeps 6-12 months. If the oil smells rancid, discard.
When not to use this remedy

Broken skin, mucous membranes, near the eyes. Hot, swollen, red joints. Significant skin reaction on first application. Children under seven. Readers who find the warming sensation unbearable on the test patch.
